title: Urticaria — Acute
oneLiner: Acute onset of pruritic wheals (hives) ± angioedema; common, self-limiting in <6 weeks, but can herald anaphylaxis.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Anaphylaxis** — look for: **stridor / wheeze / hoarseness + hypotension or syncope**. If present: treat with IM adrenaline before anything else.
- **Laryngeal angioedema** — look for: **stridor / drooling / "hot potato" voice + tongue or lip swelling without wheezing**. If present: immediate senior review, call anaesthetics ± awake fibreoptic intubation.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset of raised, erythematous, well-demarcated wheals (hives) — intensely pruritic — each lesion lasts <24 hours (usually 2–4 hours)
  - ± angioedema: deeper swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, genitalia, hands/feet (non-pitting, less pruritic, often painful)
  - Onset within minutes to hours of trigger (e.g. food, drug, insect, infection, physical stimulus)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Wheals: blanching on pressure, variable size (few mm to large plaques)
  - Excoriations from scratching
  - Check for dermographism (stroke skin with blunt object → linear wheal)
  - Vital signs normal in simple urticaria; if abnormal (tachycardia, hypotension, oxygen <94%) → treat as anaphylaxis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Acute urticaria (idiopathic / allergic)** — wheals come and go, last <24 h, no systemic signs
  - **Angioedema without urticaria** — consider ACE-inhibitor or hereditary angioedema (no pruritus, ± abdominal pain, C1 esterase inhibitor low)
  - **Viral exanthem** (e.g. EBV, parvovirus) — lesions last >24 h, associated fever/malaise
  - **Urticarial vasculitis** — wheals last >24–48 h, residual purpura/pigmentation, ± joint pain/systemic symptoms
  - **Mastocytosis** — fixed reddish-brown macules that urticate when rubbed (Darier sign), can have flushing/diarrhoea
  - **Pustular rash / erythema multiforme** — target lesions, mucosal involvement (Stevens-Johnson syndrome needs emergency dermatology)
- **Splitter:** "Wheals lasting >24 hours or leaving a bruise" → think urticarial vasculitis, not simple urticaria (send biopsy).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Assess ABC, look for stridor/hoarseness, check oxygen saturations, blood pressure
  - Dermographism test (if history suggests physical trigger)
  - No specific bedside test for routine acute urticaria
- **Bloods:**
  - If simple acute urticaria with clear history — no routine blood tests
  - If severe / anaphylactic: **mast cell tryptase** (take within 1–2 hours of onset, then at 24 hours for baseline)
  - If suspect underlying infection: FBC, CRP, viral serology (e.g. EBV IgM)
- **Imaging:**
  - None required for acute urticaria alone
  - If angioedema with suspicion of laryngeal involvement: consider CXR / lateral neck soft-tissue film (or CT) only if not improving and senior advice
- **Special tests:**
  - Skin prick testing (SPT) or specific IgE (RAST) — only after resolution, for suspected allergen identification (usually in allergy clinic)
  - Physical challenge tests (e.g. ice cube for cold urticaria) — not in acute phase
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Urticaria Severity Score / Urticaria Activity Score (UAS7) — for chronic urticaria; not needed in acute
- **Never-miss test:** **Mast cell tryptase** if there is any feature of anaphylaxis (wheals + hypotension/wheeze/stridor). Omission = missed opportunity to confirm anaphylaxis and guide future prevention.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If anaphylaxis → follow Anaphylaxis Algorithm:
    - Remove trigger (stop drug, remove sting)
    - IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1,000) anterolateral thigh – repeat if no response in 5 min
    - High-flow O₂, IV fluids (bolus 500–1000 mL crystalloid)
    - IV chlorphenamine 10 mg, IV hydrocortisone 200 mg
    - If stridor / airway compromise → immediate senior anaesthetic review
  - If simple acute urticaria (no systemic signs):
    - Oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine 10 mg, loratadine 10 mg)
    - Consider chlorphenamine 4 mg PO/IM/IV if severe itch or mild angioedema
    - Topical calamine or 1% menthol in aqueous cream for symptomatic relief
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue oral antihistamine (non-sedating) twice daily (can increase to 4× daily if needed, per local allergy guidelines)
  - If persistent severe urticaria/angioedema: prednisolone 30–40 mg once daily for 3–5 days (short course)
  - If on ward for observation post-anaphylaxis: monitor for biphasic reaction (2–8 h later); keep IV access, observe for at least 6–12 hours
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Identify and avoid triggers (diet diary, review medications)
  - If recurrent acute episodes: consider maintenance non-sedating antihistamine
  - Refer to allergy clinic if need for allergen identification / SPT
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline maintenance (unless anaphylaxis → bolus as above)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if expected inpatient stay >24 h and no contraindications
  - Diet: free, unless suspected food trigger (avoid that food); may be NBM if severe angioedema pending airway plan
  - Observations: NEWS hourly for 4–6 hours post-anaphylaxis, then 4-hourly if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (simple acute urticaria):**
  - Cetirizine 10 mg PO once daily (can increase to 20 mg BD under specialist guidance)
  - Loratadine 10 mg PO once daily
  - Chlorphenamine 4 mg PO/IM/IV every 4–6 hours (max 6 doses/24h) — short-term use only, sedating
- **For angioedema without anaphylaxis:**
  - Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV slow + hydrocortisone 200 mg IV
- **For anaphylaxis:**
  - Adrenaline IM 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1,000) — repeat every 5 min if needed; in children weight-based: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg)
  - Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV (children: 4 mg/kg, max 200 mg)
  - Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV (children: 0.25 mg/kg, max 10 mg)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Sedating antihistamines: avoid driving/machinery; caution with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines
  - Adrenaline: avoid IV unless in cardiac arrest (risk of arrhythmia); caution in patients on beta-blockers (may need glucagon)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cetirizine: dose reduce in moderate–severe renal impairment (e.g. 5 mg daily if CrCl < 30 mL/min); no adjustment in hepatic impairment
  - Loratadine: no dose adjustment needed in renal/hepatic (but caution in severe hepatic — use every other day)
  - Chlorphenamine: caution in severe hepatic impairment; no specific renal adjustment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV adrenaline when IM is indicated** — causes severe hypertension, arrhythmia, myocardial ischaemia. IM is safe and effective; IV is for cardiac arrest only.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Anaphylaxis (life-threatening)
  - Laryngeal oedema → airway obstruction
  - Syncope/fall (if hypotensive)
  - Biphasic anaphylactic reaction (hours after initial recovery)
- **Chronic:**
  - Quality of life impairment (itch, sleep loss)
  - Rare: chronic urticaria may persist >6 weeks and require specialist management
sections.Prognosis:
- Acute urticaria (no anaphylaxis) resolves within 1–2 days; recurrence depends on trigger avoidance.
- With anaphylaxis, full recovery after treatment; risk of recurrence with re-exposure — need allergy referral and autoinjector prescription.
- Mortality from anaphylaxis is rare (~1% of anaphylaxis cases) but preventable with early adrenaline.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask about new medications (including OTC NSAIDs, antibiotics, herbal remedies) and any food eaten in the last 4 hours** — most common triggers.
- **Check for angioedema (swollen lips/tongue/eyelids) in all patients with urticaria** — if present, treat as potential airway emergency.
- **For angioedema without wheals** — stop any ACE-inhibitor immediately and consider hereditary angioedema (send C1 esterase inhibitor level if recurrent/family history).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not prescribe steroids for simple acute urticaria** — antihistamines alone are sufficient; steroids are reserved for severe or refractory angioedema/anaphylaxis.
- **Do not send a routine tryptase if there is no suspicion of anaphylaxis** — waste of resource and can cause confusion.
- **Do not miss the "killer" by treating wheals without assessing airway/breathing** — always document NEWS, and never assume hypotension is "vasovagal".
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** develops difficulty breathing, swelling of lips/tongue/throat, hoarseness, dizzy/collapse, or if wheals spread rapidly despite antihistamine.
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2–4 weeks if symptoms persist; if suspected allergen identified, refer to allergy clinic (non-urgent) for testing. If had anaphylaxis, discharge with two adrenaline auto-injectors and an anaphylaxis management plan.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / crash team immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially reduced GCS, low SpO₂, hypotension)
  - Stridor, respiratory distress, oxygen saturations <92%
  - Poor response to IM adrenaline (no improvement in BP or breathing within 5 minutes)
  - Sign of biphasic reaction (worsening symptoms after initial recovery)
- Specific thresholds: SBP < 90 mmHg (adult), PEFR < 50% predicted, SpO₂ < 92% on air, GCS < 13.

rawMarkdown:
## Urticaria — Acute

**One-liner:** Acute onset of pruritic wheals (hives) ± angioedema; common, self-limiting in <6 weeks, but can herald anaphylaxis.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Anaphylaxis** — look for: **stridor / wheeze / hoarseness + hypotension or syncope**. If present: treat with IM adrenaline before anything else.
- **Laryngeal angioedema** — look for: **stridor / drooling / "hot potato" voice + tongue or lip swelling without wheezing**. If present: immediate senior review, call anaesthetics ± awake fibreoptic intubation.

### Presentation

- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset of raised, erythematous, well-demarcated wheals (hives) — intensely pruritic — each lesion lasts <24 hours (usually 2–4 hours)
  - ± angioedema: deeper swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, genitalia, hands/feet (non-pitting, less pruritic, often painful)
  - Onset within minutes to hours of trigger (e.g. food, drug, insect, infection, physical stimulus)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Wheals: blanching on pressure, variable size (few mm to large plaques)
  - Excoriations from scratching
  - Check for dermographism (stroke skin with blunt object → linear wheal)
  - Vital signs normal in simple urticaria; if abnormal (tachycardia, hypotension, oxygen <94%) → treat as anaphylaxis

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Most likely → less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Acute urticaria (idiopathic / allergic)** — wheals come and go, last <24 h, no systemic signs
  - **Angioedema without urticaria** — consider ACE-inhibitor or hereditary angioedema (no pruritus, ± abdominal pain, C1 esterase inhibitor low)
  - **Viral exanthem** (e.g. EBV, parvovirus) — lesions last >24 h, associated fever/malaise
  - **Urticarial vasculitis** — wheals last >24–48 h, residual purpura/pigmentation, ± joint pain/systemic symptoms
  - **Mastocytosis** — fixed reddish-brown macules that urticate when rubbed (Darier sign), can have flushing/diarrhoea
  - **Pustular rash / erythema multiforme** — target lesions, mucosal involvement (Stevens-Johnson syndrome needs emergency dermatology)
- **Splitter:** "Wheals lasting >24 hours or leaving a bruise" → think urticarial vasculitis, not simple urticaria (send biopsy).

### Investigations

- **Bedside:**
  - Assess ABC, look for stridor/hoarseness, check oxygen saturations, blood pressure
  - Dermographism test (if history suggests physical trigger)
  - No specific bedside test for routine acute urticaria
- **Bloods:**
  - If simple acute urticaria with clear history — no routine blood tests
  - If severe / anaphylactic: **mast cell tryptase** (take within 1–2 hours of onset, then at 24 hours for baseline)
  - If suspect underlying infection: FBC, CRP, viral serology (e.g. EBV IgM)
- **Imaging:**
  - None required for acute urticaria alone
  - If angioedema with suspicion of laryngeal involvement: consider CXR / lateral neck soft-tissue film (or CT) only if not improving and senior advice
- **Special tests:**
  - Skin prick testing (SPT) or specific IgE (RAST) — only after resolution, for suspected allergen identification (usually in allergy clinic)
  - Physical challenge tests (e.g. ice cube for cold urticaria) — not in acute phase
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Urticaria Severity Score / Urticaria Activity Score (UAS7) — for chronic urticaria; not needed in acute
- **Never-miss test:** **Mast cell tryptase** if there is any feature of anaphylaxis (wheals + hypotension/wheeze/stridor). Omission = missed opportunity to confirm anaphylaxis and guide future prevention.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If anaphylaxis → follow Anaphylaxis Algorithm:
    - Remove trigger (stop drug, remove sting)
    - IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1,000) anterolateral thigh – repeat if no response in 5 min
    - High-flow O₂, IV fluids (bolus 500–1000 mL crystalloid)
    - IV chlorphenamine 10 mg, IV hydrocortisone 200 mg
    - If stridor / airway compromise → immediate senior anaesthetic review
  - If simple acute urticaria (no systemic signs):
    - Oral non-sedating antihistamine (e.g. cetirizine 10 mg, loratadine 10 mg)
    - Consider chlorphenamine 4 mg PO/IM/IV if severe itch or mild angioedema
    - Topical calamine or 1% menthol in aqueous cream for symptomatic relief
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Continue oral antihistamine (non-sedating) twice daily (can increase to 4× daily if needed, per local allergy guidelines)
  - If persistent severe urticaria/angioedema: prednisolone 30–40 mg once daily for 3–5 days (short course)
  - If on ward for observation post-anaphylaxis: monitor for biphasic reaction (2–8 h later); keep IV access, observe for at least 6–12 hours
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Identify and avoid triggers (diet diary, review medications)
  - If recurrent acute episodes: consider maintenance non-sedating antihistamine
  - Refer to allergy clinic if need for allergen identification / SPT
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: 0.9% saline maintenance (unless anaphylaxis → bolus as above)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH if expected inpatient stay >24 h and no contraindications
  - Diet: free, unless suspected food trigger (avoid that food); may be NBM if severe angioedema pending airway plan
  - Observations: NEWS hourly for 4–6 hours post-anaphylaxis, then 4-hourly if stable

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents (simple acute urticaria):**
  - Cetirizine 10 mg PO once daily (can increase to 20 mg BD under specialist guidance)
  - Loratadine 10 mg PO once daily
  - Chlorphenamine 4 mg PO/IM/IV every 4–6 hours (max 6 doses/24h) — short-term use only, sedating
- **For angioedema without anaphylaxis:**
  - Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV slow + hydrocortisone 200 mg IV
- **For anaphylaxis:**
  - Adrenaline IM 0.5 mg (0.5 mL of 1:1,000) — repeat every 5 min if needed; in children weight-based: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg)
  - Hydrocortisone 200 mg IV (children: 4 mg/kg, max 200 mg)
  - Chlorphenamine 10 mg IV (children: 0.25 mg/kg, max 10 mg)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Sedating antihistamines: avoid driving/machinery; caution with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines
  - Adrenaline: avoid IV unless in cardiac arrest (risk of arrhythmia); caution in patients on beta-blockers (may need glucagon)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cetirizine: dose reduce in moderate–severe renal impairment (e.g. 5 mg daily if CrCl < 30 mL/min); no adjustment in hepatic impairment
  - Loratadine: no dose adjustment needed in renal/hepatic (but caution in severe hepatic — use every other day)
  - Chlorphenamine: caution in severe hepatic impairment; no specific renal adjustment
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IV adrenaline when IM is indicated** — causes severe hypertension, arrhythmia, myocardial ischaemia. IM is safe and effective; IV is for cardiac arrest only.

### Complications

- **Acute:**
  - Anaphylaxis (life-threatening)
  - Laryngeal oedema → airway obstruction
  - Syncope/fall (if hypotensive)
  - Biphasic anaphylactic reaction (hours after initial recovery)
- **Chronic:**
  - Quality of life impairment (itch, sleep loss)
  - Rare: chronic urticaria may persist >6 weeks and require specialist management

### Prognosis

- Acute urticaria (no anaphylaxis) resolves within 1–2 days; recurrence depends on trigger avoidance.
- With anaphylaxis, full recovery after treatment; risk of recurrence with re-exposure — need allergy referral and autoinjector prescription.
- Mortality from anaphylaxis is rare (~1% of anaphylaxis cases) but preventable with early adrenaline.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always ask about new medications (including OTC NSAIDs, antibiotics, herbal remedies) and any food eaten in the last 4 hours** — most common triggers.
- **Check for angioedema (swollen lips/tongue/eyelids) in all patients with urticaria** — if present, treat as potential airway emergency.
- **For angioedema without wheals** — stop any ACE-inhibitor immediately and consider hereditary angioedema (send C1 esterase inhibitor level if recurrent/family history).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not prescribe steroids for simple acute urticaria** — antihistamines alone are sufficient; steroids are reserved for severe or refractory angioedema/anaphylaxis.
- **Do not send a routine tryptase if there is no suspicion of anaphylaxis** — waste of resource and can cause confusion.
- **Do not miss the "killer" by treating wheals without assessing airway/breathing** — always document NEWS, and never assume hypotension is "vasovagal".

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:** develops difficulty breathing, swelling of lips/tongue/throat, hoarseness, dizzy/collapse, or if wheals spread rapidly despite antihistamine.
- **Follow-up:** GP in 2–4 weeks if symptoms persist; if suspected allergen identified, refer to allergy clinic (non-urgent) for testing. If had anaphylaxis, discharge with two adrenaline auto-injectors and an anaphylaxis management plan.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior / crash team immediately if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 5 (especially reduced GCS, low SpO₂, hypotension)
  - Stridor, respiratory distress, oxygen saturations <92%
  - Poor response to IM adrenaline (no improvement in BP or breathing within 5 minutes)
  - Sign of biphasic reaction (worsening symptoms after initial recovery)
- Specific thresholds: SBP < 90 mmHg (adult), PEFR < 50% predicted, SpO₂ < 92% on air, GCS < 13.
